text: Hypohydration equivalent to 2-3% body mass loss decreases endurance cycling performance in the heat when no or little fluid is ingested, based on blinded study designs that control for expectancy effects.

Many earlier studies showing hypohydration impairs endurance performance are confounded by lack of blinding, meaning subjects' knowledge of their hydration status may introduce expectancy (placebo/nocebo) effects on performance.

Methods traditionally used to induce hypohydration in studies (e.g., heat exposure, exercise-induced dehydration protocols) are uncomfortable and unfamiliar, potentially causing performance decrements from discomfort rather than dehydration itself.
The performance-impairing effect of hypohydration on endurance exercise likely operates through detrimental effects on multiple physiological functions rather than a single mechanism.
Heat conditions appear to be a key factor in whether hypohydration impairs endurance performance, with blinded studies confirming the effect specifically in hot environments.
